# Confidence Scoring and Skill Hardening with Codex

And what was that confidence score? That's something that I've just kind of built out on my own. So over time, I've kind of developed my own just confidence score for any output from either ChatGPT or Codex. But it's basically, if you, as in Codex or you as in ChatGPT, are not 100% confident in this answer, just tell me why. So give me a better sense of, okay, these are the things that would give me 100% confidence, and if it can just go look those things up, I'll have a follow-up prompt that says, "Okay, great. Look those things up to make yourself 100% confidence." Or if it's external-facing communication or internal-facing communication that I need to be 100% accurate, I'll say, "Just remove anything where you don't have 100% confidence until you get to 100% confidence." But yeah, it's basically, it's not a super quantitative metric necessarily. It's more so, okay, did you pull in every necessary resource? And then if not, what assumptions are you making so that I could remove those assumptions? Yeah. And that actually ties into the last question that I have in the chat, and then we'll wrap things up. In terms of what exactly you review and approve, have you ever thought about, and this sounds like in part the confidence score, describing what steps you take to come to a judgment and then briefing Codex on that exact approach? So where do you kind of draw that line between what the confidence score can tell you, where you want to stop and really have that human review point? No, I love that. So internally, a lot of folks will call this skill hardening. So if you have a skill and it's working well, like 85% of the time, right? I think what a lot of folks would expect is that just kind of leave it as is, and you get comfortable with that 85%, right? But I think as we all kind of become builders, like as knowledge workers start to become builders, it's going to become incumbent upon us to have more of kind of like an iterative mindset when it comes to how we build these things, right? So what I'll typically do is I'll just say, "Okay, great. This is a great output. In the future, here are three or four things that I would like you to kind of consider as a part of this skill going forward." And I'm just doing that over and over again. And then I've found that the fidelity of these use cases or of these skills over time has just significantly increased. So, if you're willing to just kind of put in the work with some of these skills and say-- I'll give an example. I have an email triage skill that responds to all of my emails every morning, or at least creates drafts for all of my emails every morning. And I'd say in the beginning, I was editing a lot of these drafts. I was like, "That's not how I sound," or, "That's not how I'd respond to this particular situation," et cetera. Or it was being overly formal if it were someone internal via Slack, and then-
